statusLine: EU average fuel stocks remain above mandatory minimums but 15 of 27 member states are below the 90-day emergency threshold in at least one fuel category, with jet fuel shortfalls the most widespread concern.
fullAnalysis:
As of June 2026, EU-wide average stock levels sit at 95.6 days for petrol, 88.1 days for diesel, and 87 days for jet fuel — placing the bloc's overall status at 'watch'. While petrol averages remain modestly above the 90-day mandatory minimum, both diesel and jet fuel averages fall below it, indicating that aggregate headline figures mask significant strain in specific fuel categories across the union. The ongoing drawdown of strategic reserves, triggered by Middle East supply disruptions since late February, is a key factor behind these shortfalls and warrants close monitoring as rebuilding those reserves will take time and capital.

At country level, the picture is considerably more acute. Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal are all rated critical across all three fuel categories, with several posting particularly low figures: Romania's diesel stands at just 30.2 days, Portugal's jet fuel at 21.2 days, and Poland's jet fuel at only 22.5 days — each well below half the mandatory threshold. Italy, Lithuania, Czechia, France, and Croatia also carry critical ratings in multiple categories. In total, 15 of 27 member states report at least one fuel type below the 90-day minimum, and the pattern of critical jet fuel readings is notably broad, affecting 19 of 27 countries. This points to aviation supply chains as a system-wide vulnerability rather than an isolated national issue.

By contrast, a group of northern and smaller member states maintain comfortable buffers. Estonia, Ireland, Latvia, and Finland report strong petrol and diesel positions, though Finland's jet fuel at 31.9 days is itself critically low — a reminder that no country is fully insulated. The Netherlands holds exceptional diesel stocks at 207.5 days while its jet fuel sits at a critical 63.2 days, illustrating how imbalances can exist within a single country's fuel portfolio. Slovenia's jet fuel figure of 365 days and Croatia's 140.5 days stand out as outliers that may partly reflect storage or reporting methodologies specific to smaller markets.

On the pricing side, the EU average sits at €1.771/L for petrol and €1.781/L for diesel as of mid-June. Brent crude is trading at $77.63/barrel, down $1.52 on the day but up 7.1% since the late-February escalation that prompted initial reserve releases. If crude prices continue to recover and strategic reserve rebuilding accelerates demand for imports, countries currently sitting in critical territory — particularly those in Central and Southern Europe — face upward pressure on both stock costs and consumer prices in the months ahead.
keyPoints: EU diesel and jet fuel averages (88.1 days and 87 days respectively) fall below the 90-day mandatory minimum, even as petrol averages (95.6 days) remain marginally above it.
15 of 27 member states are below the 90-day emergency threshold in at least one fuel category; Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal are rated critical across all three fuel types.

Jet fuel is the most widespread vulnerability: 19 of 27 countries hold critically low aviation fuel stocks, with Portugal (21.2 days), Poland (22.5 days), and Romania (30.2 days for diesel) among the most exposed.
Brent crude has risen 7.1% since the late-February supply disruption to $77.63/barrel, and ongoing strategic reserve drawdowns mean rebuilding costs will be higher for the countries most in need.

A clear north-south and east-west divide persists: Estonia, Ireland, Latvia, and Denmark maintain strong overall buffers, while Central European and Mediterranean states carry the heaviest shortfall burden.
